title: Unreal Conditional — لو (law)
explanation: لو (law) introduces hypothetical or unreal conditions — things that are unlikely, impossible, or contrary to fact. Compare with إذا (iza), which is for real possibilities. لو + past tense = 'if (hypothetically)...' The result clause often uses كان (kēn) + past verb.

rows: إذا اجا، منحكي — If he comes, we'll talk
لو اجا، كنا حكينا — If he had come, we would have talked

examples.arabic: لو كنت محلك، ما كنت عملت هيك
examples.transliteration: law kint maḥallak, mā kint ʕmilt hēk
examples.english: If I were you, I wouldn't have done that
examples.breakdown: لو (if) + كنت محلك (I were in your place) — ما كنت عملت (I wouldn't have done) + هيك (like this)

examples.arabic: لو عرفت من قبل، كنت جيت
examples.transliteration: law ʕrift min ʔabil, kint jīt
examples.english: If I had known before, I would have come
examples.breakdown: لو (if) + عرفت (I knew) + من قبل (before) — كنت جيت (I would have come)

examples.arabic: لو في وقت، كنا رحنا عالجبل
examples.transliteration: law fī waʔt, kinna ruḥna ʕal-jabal
examples.english: If there were time, we would have gone to the mountains
examples.breakdown: لو (if) + في وقت (there were time) — كنا رحنا (we would have gone)

examples.arabic: لو ما حكيت، ما كان عرف
examples.transliteration: law mā ḥkīt, mā kēn ʕirif
examples.english: If I hadn't spoken, he wouldn't have found out
examples.breakdown: لو ما (if not) + حكيت (I spoke) — ما كان عرف (he wouldn't have known)

title: Reported Speech — قال إنو / قلتلو
explanation: To report what someone said, Lebanese Arabic uses قال (ʔēl, he said) + إنو (inno, that). The verb قال takes indirect object pronoun suffixes on the preposition لـ: قلتلو (I told him), قالتلي (she told me), قالولي (they told me). إنو may be dropped in casual speech.

examples.english: I told her that I won't go in
examples.breakdown: قلتلا (I told her) + إنو (that) + مش رح فوت (I won't enter)
قالتلي (she told me) + إنا (that she) + مريضة (sick)

examples.english: We told him to wait a bit
examples.breakdown: قلنالو (we told him) + يستنى (to wait) + شوي (a bit)

title: Emphatic Structures — شو ما / وين ما / كيف ما
explanation: Adding ما (ma) after question words creates emphatic 'ever' constructions: شو ما = whatever, وين ما = wherever, كيف ما = however. These add a sense of totality or indifference. هلقد (halʔadd) means 'this much' and أد (add) means 'as much as.'

examples.breakdown: شو ما (whatever) + كان (it was/happens) — أنا معك (I'm with you)

examples.english: Wherever you go, you find Lebanese people
examples.breakdown: وين ما (wherever) + رحت (you went) — بتلاقي (you find) + لبنانيين (Lebanese)

examples.english: However you want / any way you like
I didn't expect it to be this nice
examples.breakdown: ما كنت (I wasn't) + متوقع (expecting) + هلقد (this much) + حلو (nice)

examples.english: No matter how much you try, it stays difficult
examples.breakdown: أد ما (as much as) + بتحاول (you try) — بيضل (it stays) + صعب (difficult)

title: Discourse Markers — The Glue of Fluency
explanation: Lebanese conversation is held together by small words that signal attitude, transition, or emphasis. Mastering these is what separates textbook Arabic from real Lebanese speech. They appear constantly and carry social as much as grammatical meaning.

rows: Starting a new topic or agreeing
Ending a topic or expressing finality

examples.english: Okay, so what do we do now?
examples.breakdown: طيب (okay) + هلق (now) — two markers opening a transition
خلص (done/enough) + يلا (let's go) — closing and moving on

examples.english: That's the deal, I mean it's not hard
examples.breakdown: هيك (like this) + يعني (I mean) — casual summarizing
